summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for project postmortems as essential learning tools, using game development as a prime example. He highlights Game Developer Magazine's '5 wrongs, 5 rights' framework as an effective template for conducting retrospectives. The post emphasizes that postmortems should be given high priority—significant enough to be cover stories. Atwood argues that game development postmortems are particularly valuable because the industry's pressure-cooker environment surfaces every possible project pathology, making lessons transferable to all software projects.
key_points: The '5 wrongs, 5 rights' framework provides a balanced structure for honest project retrospectives
Postmortems should be treated as high-priority activities, not afterthoughts
Game development serves as an extreme case study because it combines unclear goals ('fun') with intense deadlines
Learning from others' failures is even more valuable than learning from your own
Honest assessment of failures is more valuable than sugar-coated retrospectives—publications reject postmortems that aren't candid
money_quotes: If you're not learning from your mistakes, or even better, learning from other people's mistakes, then your next project will have a rocky future at best.
Game development is such a pressure cooker.
It's incredibly challenging software engineering, with unclear goals (e.g., 'fun') under intense deadlines.
Every project pathology you're likely to see on your software project has probably already materialized on one or more of these games.
Postmortems that accentuate the 'what went right' and sugar coat the 'what went wrong' sections are dismissed by readers.
It's instructive to soak in the successes as well as gawk at the trainwrecks.
key_insight: Game development postmortems are universally valuable because the industry's extreme pressure surfaces every project pathology that can afflict software development.
